system_prompt:
You are deriving structured "systems" from the distilled summary of a guitar method book.

A SYSTEM is a graph-with-rules. It has:
  - members[]: the taxonomy (the things this system operates over — chord
    types, position regions, voicing categories, scale forms, etc.)
  - traversal_rules[]: how you MOVE between members (preference for
    common-tone voice motion, pivot-finger shifts, position continuity,
    etc.)
  - modification_rules[]: how you ALTER a member (substitution lattices,
    voicing density changes, omission policies, color-tone requirements,
    etc.)

Each rule carries an `engine_payload` with a `kind` field. The kind is
either ONE of these 12 names (use exactly as spelled):

  PositionContinuity, VoiceMotion, StringSetTransition, SymmetryMovement,
  FamilyCoherence, SubstitutionExpand, DensityFloor, DensityCeiling,
  OmissionAllow, ColorToneRequire, NCTHarmonization, TextureCycle

OR a `_pending:<short-kebab-slug>` placeholder when no name in the list
clearly fits. When in doubt, USE `_pending:`. Do not stretch a name to fit.
The formal definitions of these kinds are pending (#298); your job today
is to pick the best label or honestly admit uncertainty.

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  {
    "master_id": "<from config>",
    "work_id": "<from config>",
    "systems": [
      {
        "id": "<master_id>:<work_id>:<system-slug>",
        "name": "<full system name>",
        "summary": "<one-paragraph prose>",
        "members": [
          { "id": "<slug>", "name": "<full name>", "summary": "<optional>" }
        ],
        "traversal_rules": [
          {
            "id": "<slug>",
            "name": "<full name>",
            "summary": "<one sentence>",
            "engine_payload": { "kind": "<kind-name or _pending:slug>" }
          }
        ],
        "modification_rules": [...]
      }
    ]
  }

Guidance:
- Three-segment system ids. Slug is kebab-case. Example:
  "benson:method-vol-1-chord-construction:harmonized-scale"
- Aim for 1-4 systems per book. A book usually has ONE central system plus
  optionally a few smaller ones.
- A system MUST have non-empty members AND at least one rule (traversal
  or modification) — empty systems are not useful.
- members[], traversal_rules[], modification_rules[] are all optional fields
  per the schema, but a real system has at least members + one rule kind.
- If the book teaches a system you can't structure as members+rules,
  describe it in `summary` and leave members/rules empty rather than
  inventing.

EVERY rule (traversal_rule, modification_rule, preference) MUST carry a
`references[]` array with at least one entry pointing back at the chapter
quote that backs the rule. Reference shape:

  { "chapter_n": <int>, "topic": "<topic label from chapter file>",
    "quote_excerpt": "<5-15 word snippet of the source quote>",
    "page": <int, optional> }

If a rule has no source quote it does not belong in the systems-draft —
write it into the system's `summary` field or omit it. References make
every kind assignment auditable; without them, this draft cannot be
reviewed against the source.

# Book-level distillation — jazz-standards-playbook

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

This book teaches jazz improvisation through sequential study of ten carefully chosen jazz standards, using each tune as a vehicle for introducing and reinforcing a cumulative set of harmonic, melodic, and practice principles. The pedagogical architecture is explicitly ear-first: Chapter 1 establishes the LIST process (Listen, Internalize, Sing, Transfer) as the foundational method, positioning sheet music as a verification tool rather than a primary source, and centering effortful aural retrieval—singing melodies, bass lines, and chord roots before touching the instrument—as the engine of durable musical memory. Every subsequent chapter applies this orientation, grounding new harmonic concepts in specific tunes before abstracting them into transferable principles.

The book's harmonic vocabulary builds systematically across the chapters. The Major and Minor Diatonic Series of 7th Chords, introduced in Chapter 2 through Autumn Leaves, provides the foundational framework for reading scale degrees and chord qualities. The ii-V-I progression is declared the cornerstone of jazz harmony in that same chapter and reappears in virtually every subsequent analysis. Guide tones—the 3rds and 7ths of chords—are established early as the primary melodic spine of improvisation and the defining intervals for chord quality, and voice leading (smooth stepwise motion of guide tones across chord changes) is presented as the governing principle for melodic connection. Secondary dominants and tonicization appear in Chapter 4 (All of Me), where the master introduces the relational principle that a chord's function depends on context—the chords before and after it—not on its intrinsic quality in isolation. Chapters 5 through 9 elaborate this harmonic system through target chords, tritone substitution, chromatic passing chords, diminished 7th chords as rootless dominant b9 substitutes, and the Whole-Half diminished scale. Chapter 9 (Have You Met Miss Jones) introduces the cycle of major thirds as an analytical device for navigating rapid modulations, while Chapter 10 (All the Things You Are) consolidates cycling fourths as a foundational harmonic movement throughout the jazz repertoire.

A parallel strand of modal and chromatic theory runs through the later chapters. Chapter 6 introduces the Mixolydian scale—defined as a major scale with a flatted 7th (1-2-3-4-5-6-b7)—and reframes scales not as linear exercises but as pitch collections: harmonic resource pools from which melodic choices are drawn over chord changes. Chapter 7 (So What) introduces modal harmony as a distinct system from functional harmony, teaching Dorian mode (W-H-W-W-W-H-W) and the principle that guide tones are unnecessary in modal contexts. The master models Miles Davis's sparse, space-conscious approach to modal playing as the idiomatic application of modal knowledge, warning against mechanical scale-running in favor of selective note choice. Throughout both functional and modal contexts, the book prescribes taking all licks and patterns through all 12 keys as the concrete internalization method.

The book establishes several named analytical tools and reductive labels as characteristic moves. The backdoor dominant (bVII7 substituting for V7 by whole-step approach) and its extension, the backdoor ii-V, appear in Chapters 6 and 11. The hybrid ii-V-I—mixing minor ii-V from a minor key while resolving to a major chord—is introduced in Chapter 11 (Stella by Starlight) as an expressive harmonic surprise. The cycle-of-fourths functions as both an analytical tool for identifying substitution relationships and a compositional pattern observable across multiple standards. The minor-third key-center shift is identified in Chapter 8 as a recurring compositional pattern. The master's characteristic move throughout is to attach memorable labels to complex harmonic phenomena—backdoor, tritone substitution, tonicization—making analytical recognition faster and improvisation decisions more immediate.

Improvisation method is taught through a layered set of concrete practice protocols rather than abstract principles. Chapter 3's three-chorus melody exercise scaffolds from playing melody straight, to embellishing it as a guideline, to freely improvising while referencing melodic fragments—making the tune's identity audible even during extended departures. Chapter 8 prescribes playing as few notes as possible, sometimes one note over an entire sequence, to isolate chord-tone hearing. Chapter 11 introduces quarter-note solo exercises to separate harmonic navigation from rhythmic complexity, and note-mapping to conceptualize available pitch choices. Chapter 5 introduces the contrafact—a new melody composed over an existing song's chord changes—as a compositional practice tool, with two forms: a simple singable melody outlining chord changes and a busier bebop-style head with deliberate structural repetition. Chapter 10 consolidates six core improvisation tools: guide tones, melody, chord tones, contrafacts, motifs, and transcribed licks, adding composed solos as a method for slowing improvisation down to develop jazz language.

The master's characteristic aesthetic principles, stated explicitly across multiple chapters, include: strong melody licenses harmonic freedom including outside playing, provided lines resolve with intention (Chapter 3); improvisation is composition accelerated (Chapter 5); space and melodic breathing are hallmarks of strong improvisation (Chapter 5); simplicity over crowding governs harmonic navigation in dense passages (Chapter 8); and the defining-notes question—what are the defining notes of each chord, and which intervals (especially 3rds and 7ths) must change?—serves as the analytical tool for any harmonically confusing passage (Chapter 8). The book's closing chapter frames each standard not as an isolated piece but as an exemplar of harmonic principles transferable across hundreds of other tunes, positioning the standards themselves as vehicles—platforms for improvisation exploration—and characterizing music as a lifetime study without a final arrival point, reorienting the student from mastery toward perpetual exploration.

# Chapter 1 — Before We Start: The Pro Guide to Learning Jazz Standards

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch01.md`._

Chapter 1 establishes ear-first learning as the foundational pedagogical method for jazz standards, introducing the LIST process (Listen, Internalize, Sing, Transfer) as the book's central framework for aural acquisition. The chapter teaches that jazz musicians must prioritize listening to multiple recordings, practicing intentional undivided attention, singing melodies and bass lines as proof of internalization, and then transferring that knowledge to the instrument through melody first, followed by chord learning via root motion and quality identification. Key techniques include learning vocal versions and lyrics to deepen melodic memory, using Frank Sinatra as a reference for straight melody renditions, and isolating bass notes to train harmonic ear training. The master's characteristic move is reframing sheet music not as a primary learning tool but as a checking mechanism, positioning effortful retrieval through repetition—rather than notation—as the engine of lasting musical retention and real-time improvisational responsiveness.

The chapter establishes the Major and Minor Diatonic Series of 7th Chords as the primary framework for reading scale degrees and chord qualities, clarifies the convention of "concert keys" as the standard performance pitches for jazz standards, and defines the relative key relationship that governs Autumn Leaves' harmonic structure. The master's characteristic move throughout is the emphasis on practical jazz conventions—such as the modal mixture of minor IV to dominant seventh and the pervasive ii-V-I progression—over strict classical theory. The chapter introduces three load-bearing terms: guide tones (the 3rds and 7ths of chords, which define quality and serve as the melodic spine of improvisation), voice leading (the smooth stepwise motion of these tones from chord to chord), and the ii-V-I progression, declared the "cornerstone" of jazz harmony that students must recognize and improvise over immediately. The chapter concludes with a concrete practice method: playing guide tones isolated in half or quarter notes through the entire song form.

# Chapter 4 — Jazz Standard Study #3: All of Me

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch04.md`._

Chapter 4 teaches harmonic analysis of the jazz standard "All of Me" through the lens of chord function and voice leading. The central principle is that a chord's function depends on its harmonic context—both the chords before and after it—establishing that analysis is relational rather than absolute. Key concepts introduced include secondary dominants and tonicization (when a dominant 7 chord acts as V of a non-tonic diatonic chord), explicit formulas for major 7, dominant 7, minor 7, half-diminished, and diminished 7 chords, and the chromatic approach as a named technique for non-structural melodic movement. The chapter reinforces that chord tones (particularly guide tones: 3rds and 7ths) rather than scales are the primary material for improvisation, and establishes a voice-leading rule for chord tone arpeggios: they always move in order (Root-3-5-7 or any rotation), with smooth connection across chord changes determined by proximity to the next chord. Characteristic moves include analyzing the IV-to-iv minor pivot as tonicization into C minor and introducing the cycle-of-fourths as an analytical tool for substitution chords, while cautioning against major intervals when improvising over iv minor chords.

# Chapter 7 — Jazz Standard Study #6: So What

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch07.md`._

Chapter 7 introduces modal harmony through the jazz standard "So What," a foundational departure from the functional harmony that dominates earlier standards in the book. The chapter establishes that modal harmony operates on modes derived from a parent major scale—specifically, D Dorian (built from C major) with a brief modulation to Eb Dorian (from Db major)—and teaches students that guide tones are unnecessary in modal contexts. The core techniques presented include the Dorian mode formula (W-H-W-W-W-H-W), voice-leading principles for modal arpeggios that connect via the nearest chord tone rather than always starting on the root, and modal pattern exercises in thirds that link adjacent modes through half- and whole-step intervals. Rather than mechanically running scales, students learn to use modes as note choices within improvisation. The chapter distills Miles Davis's characteristic approach to this tune: sparse, space-conscious playing that stays primarily within the Dorian mode while occasionally employing chromatic approach notes, modeling how to apply modal knowledge idiomatically rather than literally.

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch09.md`._

Chapter 9 applies harmonic substitution and voice-leading techniques to the jazz standard "Have You Met Miss Jones," focusing on how diminished 7 chords function both as rootless dominant b9 substitutes and as chromatic passing chords. # Chapter 10 — Jazz Standard Study #9: All the Things You Are

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch10.md`._

Chapter 10 analyzes "All the Things You Are," a 36-bar AABA jazz standard in Ab major that serves as an intensive study of cycling 4ths—a foundational harmonic movement in jazz. The chapter teaches how the tune opens with a vi chord that initiates a diatonic cycle of 4ths, how key transpositions repeat the first eight bars in new centers (with the V chord acting as the "secret weapon" for resolving to new keys), and how passing diminished chords function chromatically to connect chord changes. Jerome Kern's harmonic choices in bars 29-32 deliberately depart from pure cycle-of-4ths motion, offering players an alternative conceptual framework for non-diatonic passages. Beyond harmonic analysis, the chapter consolidates six core improvisation tools—guide tones, melody, chord tones, contrafacts, motifs, and transcribed licks—and emphasizes composed solos as a deliberate practice method to slow improvisation down and develop jazz language, whether notated or memorized directly on the instrument.

> Jazz is first and foremost
> aurally learned music. So if you want to become a great jazz im-
> proviser, you need to follow in the footsteps of the great jazz musi-
> cians before you.

**Significance (p. 12).** This is the book's foundational pedagogical rule — ear-first learning over sheet music — which governs every subsequent method recommendation.

## Sheet music proper role

> The idea is not to throw away sheet music but to know its place. If
> you genuinely want to become great jazz improviser, you are going
> to need to get your face out of the pages and open up your ears
> instead.

**Significance (p. 12).** This clarifies the prescriptive rule: sheet music is a checking tool, not a learning primary, establishing the hierarchy students must internalize.

## Ear learning: internalization benefit

> You have to listen to small fragments of music at a
> time and figure it out on your instrument. This naturally results
> in many repetitions. In short, the harder you work for it, the more
> likely you are to remember it.

**Significance (p. 13).** This explains the cognitive mechanism behind ear-first learning — effortful retrieval via repetition — giving students a reason to adopt the harder method.

## Ear learning: reaction/improvisation benefit

> This is where sharpening your ear comes in. Let’s say you are
> playing a jazz standard and a ii-V-| chord progression is coming
> up in the form (in the key of C: Dmin7-G7-Cmaj7).
>
> But suddenly you hear the piano player approach it chromati-
> cally (Ebmin7-Ab7-Dmin7-G7-Cmaj7). What do you do? Do you
> double check the sheet music to see if the piano player is play-
> ing the “correct” chord changes, and then scold him or her for
> getting it wrong? Of course not.

**Significance (p. 13).** This concrete example of a chromatically substituted ii-V-I demonstrates why ear training is non-negotiable for real-time jazz performance.

> Listen
> Internalize
> Sing
>
> Transfer

**Significance (p. 15).** This is the book's central procedural framework — the four-step acronym that structures all subsequent ear-learning instruction.

## Listen step: instrument away

> The most important thing you can do when learning jazz standards is
> put your instrument away and just listen. Find as many recordings as
> possible of the jazz standard you want to learn and go through all of

**Significance (p. 15).** This gives the operative rule for the Listen step — deliberate, instrument-free listening across multiple recordings before any playing begins.

> ther the Major or Minor Diatonic Series of 7“ chords. We have seen ii-

> somewhere else. The key center is being eluded to, yet never comes
> home.

**Significance (p. 177).** Identifies the defining harmonic characteristic of Stella's opening — tonally evasive unresolved ii-Vs — which governs how the entire song should be understood and navigated.

## backdoor ii-V concept

> The ii chord is being added in front of the backdoor dominant. There-
> fore, the Bbmin7-Eb7 is really acting as a backdoor ii-V into the Fmaj7.

**Significance (p. 178).** Extends the backdoor dominant concept to the full backdoor ii-V, showing how a preceding ii chord can be incorporated into the substitution pattern.

## note map as improv tool

> When we come across songs like Stella, it can be beneficial to take a
> step back and map out the notes we have available to us. While scales
> are not musical by themselves, they can help us conceptualize which
> notes we can choose from. From there, we can start to build melodies
> and make sense of how to connect the harmony in our lines.

**Significance (p. 181).** Introduces the note-mapping approach as a conceptual scaffolding tool for improvisation over harmonically dense material.

## quarter note solo exercise

> One exercise | like to do to keep things simple and help me start to
> improvise is create quarter note solos. This means | stick to a quarter
> note rhythm in all of my note choices. | take out the rhythmic compo-
> nent from my improvisation and just focus on navigating the changes.

**Significance (p. 184).** Prescribes a specific practice technique that isolates harmonic navigation from rhythm, reducing cognitive load when learning to improvise over complex changes.

## cycle of 4ths minor ii-Vs

> Bar 25 marks the last section of the song. The first two bars are the
> same as the beginning of the tune, but this time we continue on with a
> cycle of 4ths. This cycle of 4ths is different than we have seen before
> in that they are a series of minor ii-V’s.

**Significance (p. 179).** Distinguishes the closing cycle-of-4ths progression as built from minor ii-Vs rather than dominant ii-Vs, a structural distinction that affects scale choice during improvisation.

## Ab7 dual interpretation

> But there are two ways to perceive the Ab7 in bar 8. You can think of
> it as a V7 of the IV chord (fairly common), or you can recognize it as
> a backdoor dominant moving into the | chord in bar 10.

**Significance (p. 177).** Models dual harmonic interpretation of a single chord, teaching the reader to hold multiple functional readings simultaneously rather than locking into one.

## Jazz standards as vehicles

> Jazz standards are the vehicles that jazz musicians use to improvise.
> To become a better jazz musician, it is imperative that we study them
> and explore their unique harmonic worlds. The more jazz standards you
> explore, the more you will understand how to improvise over all of them.

**Significance (p. 188).** This passage states the book's core pedagogical premise: that jazz standards are the primary medium through which improvisation is learned and developed.

## Standards chosen for harmonic lessons

> | chose them because they all con-
> tain powerful harmonic and rhythmic lessons that apply to hundreds of
> other standards.

**Significance (p. 188).** This names the selection criterion — transferability of harmonic and rhythmic lessons — which frames the entire book's methodology.

## Music as lifetime study

> Indeed, music is a life-
> time study. There is no final arrival point.

**Significance (p. 188).** This is a rule-of-practice framing that orients the student's long-term relationship with the material beyond this single text.
